result.status_note: The Birch-Tate conjecture relates the order of the K_2 group of the ring of integers of a number field F to the value of its Dedekind zeta function at s=-1. It has been proven for abelian number fields (via the Iwasawa Main Conjecture, e.g., by Mazur-Wiles for Q and Wiles for totally real fields at odd primes, building on Rubin's work). The general case for arbitrary number fields, especially at p=2, remains open and very challenging. It is a special case of the more general Beilinson-Bloch conjecture and the Tamagawa Number Conjecture.
result.approach_sketch: The core strategy involves connecting algebraic K-theory groups (specifically K_2(O_F)) to Galois cohomology groups. For abelian fields, this connection is established via Iwasawa theory, relating the K_2 group to p-adic L-functions and the Iwasawa Main Conjecture. The conjecture essentially states an equality between an arithmetic invariant (order of K_2) and an analytic invariant (zeta function value), often mediated by a p-adic regulator and a 'main conjecture' relating arithmetic and analytic objects.
key_references: Birch, Tate (1971) - Original formulation of the conjecture
Quillen, D. (1973) - Higher algebraic K-theory
Mazur, Wiles (1984) - Proof of the Main Conjecture of Iwasawa Theory for Q
Wiles, A. (1990) - The Iwasawa Main Conjecture for totally real fields
Rubin, K. (1991) - The 'main conjecture' for imaginary quadratic fields
Bloch, Kato (1990) - L-functions and Tamagawa numbers of motives
result.connections: The Birch-Tate conjecture is a foundational problem in number theory, deeply connected to the Iwasawa Main Conjecture, the Bloch-Kato Conjecture, the Beilinson-Bloch Conjecture, and the overarching Tamagawa Number Conjecture. It relates to the structure of K-theory groups of rings of integers, special values of L-functions, and arithmetic invariants.
result.assessment: A full resolution of the Birch-Tate conjecture for arbitrary number fields, particularly the challenging p=2 case, is unlikely within the next 10 years. Its complete proof would likely require a significant breakthrough in the general framework of the Tamagawa Number Conjecture or the Bloch-Kato Conjecture, which are themselves extremely hard and central problems. However, further partial results for specific classes of fields or specific primes are certainly possible and expected, building on advances in Iwasawa theory and related areas.

result.status_note: The Connes embedding problem (CEP) is now known to have a negative answer. This was established by a sequence of breakthroughs, most notably the disproof of Tsirelson's problem (which is equivalent to CEP) by Ji, Natarajan, Vidick, Wright, and Yuen in 2020, using techniques from computational complexity theory (MIP*=RE).
result.approach_sketch: The problem, originally stated in terms of embedding separable II_1 factors into ultrapowers of the hyperfinite II_1 factor R, was shown to be equivalent to various other conjectures, including the matricial approximation property for tracial von Neumann algebras and the equality of two sets of quantum correlations (C_co and C_ts) in quantum information theory (Tsirelson's problem). The disproof involved constructing a quantum interactive proof system (MIP*) whose power was shown to be equivalent to recursively enumerable sets (RE), which allowed for the construction of a specific quantum game that separates C_co from C_ts, thereby providing a negative answer to Tsirelson's problem and, by equivalence, to CEP.
key_techniques: Von Neumann algebra theory (II_1 factors, property Gamma, ultrapowers)
Operator space theory (local reflexivity, exactness of C*-algebras)
Quantum information theory (Bell inequalities, quantum correlations, Tsirelson's problem)
Computational complexity theory (MIP*=RE, quantum interactive proof systems)
key_references: Connes 1976 - Classification of Injective Factors (original formulation)
Kirchberg 1993 - On the exactness of C*-algebras and the embedding problem (established equivalences)
Ozawa 2004 - About the Connes embedding problem (established further equivalences)
Fritz, Junge, Palazuelos, Vidick 2011 - Quantum correlations and the Connes embedding problem (connected to Tsirelson's problem)
Ji, Natarajan, Vidick, Wright, Yuen 2020 - MIP*=RE (disproved Tsirelson's problem, thus CEP)
result.connections: The Connes embedding problem is deeply connected to numerous areas: the structure theory of von Neumann algebras, C*-algebra exactness and local reflexivity, non-commutative L_p spaces, free probability, Grothendieck's inequality, hyperlinearity of groups, and quantum information theory (Tsirelson's problem). Its resolution has profound implications for the foundations of quantum mechanics and computational complexity.
result.assessment: The Connes embedding problem is now considered solved in the negative. This was a monumental achievement, bridging deep results in operator algebras with quantum information theory and theoretical computer science. While the original problem statement has a definitive answer, the implications of this negative resolution, such as the existence of non-hyperlinear groups and the separation of different models of quantum correlations, will continue to drive research in operator algebras, quantum information, and related fields for many years to come. The full ramifications are still being explored.

result.status_note: The conjecture states that the constant is 1. Crouzeix proved in 2007 that the constant is at most 2 for any matrix A and any analytic function f. More recently, Crouzeix and Greenbaum (2023) showed that the constant is at most sqrt(2) for normal matrices. The original conjecture (constant 1) remains open.
result.approach_sketch: The problem connects the operator norm of a function of a matrix, f(A), to the supremum of f over the numerical range (field of values) of A, denoted W(A). Key approaches involve using properties of the resolvent operator (A-zI)^-1, functional calculus for analytic functions, and geometric properties of the numerical range. The proof for the constant 2 relies on a specific integral representation involving the resolvent and conformal mappings. Progress for normal matrices leverages the spectral theorem and the fact that W(A) for normal A is the convex hull of its eigenvalues.
key_references: Crouzeix, M. (2007). The numerical range and numerical radius: an overview. In Numerical range and numerical radius (pp. 1-13). American Mathematical Society.
Crouzeix, M. (2004). Some remarks on the numerical range of a matrix. Linear Algebra and its Applications, 383, 11-21.
Crouzeix, M., & Greenbaum, A. (2023). The Crouzeix conjecture for normal matrices. arXiv preprint arXiv:2303.04544.
result.connections: This conjecture has deep connections to numerical analysis, particularly in understanding the stability and convergence of iterative methods for linear systems and eigenvalue problems. It relates to the theory of pseudospectra, operator theory, and complex analysis, especially in the context of functional calculus and conformal mappings. It also has implications for control theory and the stability of dynamical systems.
result.assessment: This problem is genuinely hard and has resisted solution for many years. While significant progress has been made in establishing bounds (constant 2, and sqrt(2) for normal matrices), closing the gap to 1 is a formidable challenge. It is possible that a breakthrough could occur within the next 10 years, perhaps through novel insights into the geometry of the numerical range or new techniques from complex analysis. However, it would require a substantial conceptual leap, as the current methods seem to hit a wall at the existing bounds. A counterexample, if one exists, would also be a major discovery.

result.status_note: The Eilenberg-Ganea conjecture, posed in 1957, remains a major open problem in homological algebra and algebraic topology. It is known to hold for certain classes of groups (e.g., free groups, surface groups, groups with finite classifying spaces), but the general case is unresolved.
result.approach_sketch: The conjecture states that if a group G has cohomological dimension 2 (cd(G)=2), then G admits a 2-dimensional Eilenberg-MacLane space K(G,1). This is equivalent to showing that if the trivial G-module Z has a projective resolution of length 2 over the group ring ZG, then it also has a free resolution of length 2. A proof would likely involve constructing such a free resolution from a given projective one, possibly by demonstrating that projective ZG-modules are free in this specific context, or by constructing a 2-dimensional CW complex directly. A counterexample would require constructing a group G with cd(G)=2 for which all 2-dimensional CW complexes with fundamental group G have non-trivial higher homotopy groups.
key_references: Eilenberg, S., & Ganea, T. (1957) - On the Lusternik-Schnirelmann category of a space (original statement)
Brown, K. S. (1982) - Cohomology of Groups (standard reference for cohomological dimension)
Wall, C. T. C. (1966) - Finiteness conditions for CW-complexes (related work on classifying spaces)
Geoghegan, R. (2008) - Topological Methods in Group Theory (discusses the conjecture and related problems)
result.connections: This conjecture is deeply connected to the structure of projective modules over group rings, particularly the Bass conjecture and Kaplansky's conjecture on the freeness of projective modules. It also relates to the Whitehead conjecture (on subcomplexes of aspherical complexes) and the general theory of finiteness properties of groups (FP_n, FG, FP). It has implications for geometric group theory, particularly for groups acting on low-dimensional spaces.
result.assessment: This is a very hard, long-standing open problem that connects fundamental concepts in homological algebra and algebraic topology. A resolution, whether positive or negative, would likely require a significant breakthrough in our understanding of projective modules over group rings or in the construction and analysis of low-dimensional CW complexes. While progress on specific classes of groups or related conjectures is always possible, a full resolution of the general conjecture within the next 10 years seems unlikely without a major new conceptual tool or technique emerging in the field.

result.status_note: The Eilenberg-Ganea conjecture remains open in general. It has been proven for many important classes of groups, including free groups, surface groups, one-relator groups (Lyndon, Dunwoody), groups with finite virtual cohomological dimension 2 (Bieri, Strebel), and groups with cd(G)=2 over a field (Stallings). However, the general case for arbitrary groups of cohomological dimension 2 is still unknown.
result.approach_sketch: The problem is to construct a 2-dimensional CW complex X such that its fundamental group π_1(X) is G and its higher homotopy groups π_k(X) are trivial for k > 1. Given that G has cohomological dimension 2, there exists a 2-dimensional projective resolution of Z over ZG. The challenge is to geometrically realize this algebraic information, specifically to show that the second homology group H_2(G,Z) can be killed by attaching 2-cells without introducing higher-dimensional homotopy groups, which is not guaranteed by the cohomological dimension condition alone. This involves constructing a suitable presentation for G and realizing it as a 2-complex.
key_references: Eilenberg, S., Ganea, T. (1957) - On the Lusternik-Schnirelmann category of a space (original paper stating the conjecture).
Lyndon, R.C. (1950) - On the cohomology of one-relator groups (proof for one-relator groups).
Dunwoody, M.J. (1979) - The homotopy type of a two-dimensional complex (generalizes Lyndon's result for one-relator groups).
Stallings, J.R. (1968) - On torsion-free groups with finite cohomological dimension (related results, e.g., for cd=1).
Bieri, R., Strebel, R. (1980) - A geometric invariant for modules over group rings (related to virtual cohomological dimension).
Brown, K.S. (1982) - Cohomology of Groups (standard reference for background on group cohomology).
result.connections: This conjecture lies at the intersection of homological algebra, algebraic topology, and geometric group theory. It connects the algebraic invariant of cohomological dimension (cd(G)) with the topological invariant of geometric dimension (gd(G), the minimum dimension of a K(G,1) space). The conjecture states that if cd(G)=2, then gd(G)=2. It is related to the study of group presentations, the construction of classifying spaces, and the general problem of realizing algebraic properties geometrically. It has implications for understanding the structure of groups with low cohomological dimension and the relationship between algebraic and topological invariants.
result.assessment: It is unlikely to be solved in the next 10 years. The conjecture has been open for over 60 years, and while significant progress has been made for specific classes of groups, the general case remains elusive. A solution would likely require a breakthrough in understanding the relationship between algebraic and geometric properties of groups, possibly involving new techniques in homotopy theory or a deeper understanding of the structure of projective resolutions over group rings. Finding a counterexample would also be a major achievement, requiring the construction of a group with cd(G)=2 that provably cannot be realized as the fundamental group of a 2-dimensional CW complex, which is a very difficult task.

result.status_note: The conjecture is known to be true for torsion-free groups (Dunwoody, 1979) and for groups of type FP_2 (Wall, 1965). The general case, particularly for groups with torsion that are not of type FP_2, remains open.
result.approach_sketch: The core idea is to construct a 2-dimensional CW complex K(G,1) from the algebraic data of a projective resolution of Z over ZG of length 2. This involves realizing the generators and relations of G as 1-cells and 2-cells, respectively, and ensuring that all higher homotopy groups are trivial. The challenge lies in handling the higher-order syzygies and ensuring the universal cover is contractible, especially when torsion is present and the group is not of type FP_2.
key_techniques: Homological algebra (projective resolutions, cohomological dimension, chain complexes)
Algebraic topology (CW complexes, Eilenberg-MacLane spaces, homotopy theory, universal covers)
Group theory (group presentations, relations, syzygies, torsion)
Whitehead's theorem (on realizing chain complexes by spaces)
key_references: Eilenberg, S., & Ganea, T. (1957) - Original statement of the conjecture
Wall, C. T. C. (1965) - Finiteness conditions for CW-complexes, relating to FP_n and finite K(G,1)s
Dunwoody, M. J. (1979) - Proof for torsion-free groups
Brown, K. S. (1982) - Cohomology of Groups (standard reference for cd and related topics)
result.connections: This conjecture is deeply connected to the finiteness properties of groups (FP_n, FG, FH), Whitehead's asphericity conjecture, the deficiency of groups, and geometric group theory (e.g., groups acting on 2-complexes). It touches on fundamental questions about the interplay between algebraic and topological properties of groups.
result.assessment: Unlikely to be solved in the next 10 years. The problem has resisted attacks for over 60 years, and the remaining open cases (groups with torsion that are not FP_2) are notoriously difficult. A solution would likely require a significant new conceptual tool or a breakthrough in understanding the interplay between torsion, homological algebra, and geometric realization.

result.status_note: The conjecture is a major open problem in arithmetic differential equations. It has been proven for curves (dimension 1) by André and Bost. Significant partial results exist for certain classes of D-modules and varieties in higher dimensions, but the general case remains open.
result.approach_sketch: The conjecture relates the nilpotence of p-curvature for almost all primes p (a local arithmetic property) to the 'algebraicity' of a differential equation (a global geometric property, e.g., having a finite differential Galois group). The 'if' direction (nilpotent p-curvature implies algebraic solutions) is the harder part. Approaches often involve constructing a global Frobenius structure, using deformation theory to lift solutions from characteristic p to characteristic 0, or employing techniques from rigid analytic geometry and model theory (especially for curves). The goal is to show that the differential Galois group is finite.
key_references: Katz, N. M. (1970s-1980s) - Foundational work on p-curvature and D-modules.
André, Y. (1989, 2001) - Proof of the conjecture for curves.
Bost, J.-B. (1994) - Connections to model theory and proof for curves.
Esnault, H., & Viehweg, E. (1990s) - Related work on vanishing cycles and D-modules.
Kedlaya, K. (2000s) - Work on p-adic differential equations and Frobenius structures.
result.connections: This conjecture is deeply connected to differential Galois theory, arithmetic geometry (especially reduction modulo p and Frobenius maps), the theory of D-modules, and p-adic Hodge theory. It can be seen as an arithmetic analogue of the global nilpotence theorem in complex geometry (which relates local nilpotence of monodromy to global properties). It also has connections to the theory of motives and non-abelian Hodge theory.
result.assessment: Unlikely to be fully solved in the next 10 years. The generalization from curves to higher dimensions has proven extremely challenging, requiring fundamentally new ideas beyond current techniques. While significant partial results for specific classes of D-modules or varieties are certainly plausible, a complete resolution of the general conjecture would likely require a breakthrough in our understanding of D-modules and Frobenius structures in higher dimensions, possibly involving new tools from p-adic geometry or deformation theory.
